title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita, 2024
Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of workers in informal employment and GDP per capita for countries in 2024, with the horizontal axis measuring GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale and the vertical axis measuring the percentage of workers in informal employment. Circles represent individual countries, sized by population, and colored by region. The data points form a distinct downward curve from the upper left to the lower right, showing that countries with lower GDP per capita have higher shares of informal employment. In the upper left, countries with GDP per capita below $5,000 show informal employment shares above 60%: DR Congo, Chad, Tanzania, Ethiopia, Somalia, Pakistan, Ghana, Vanuatu, and Tajikistan all fall in this range. India, represented by the largest circle on the chart, sits near $10,000 GDP per capita with an informal employment share around 85%. Moving rightward, countries in the $10,000 to $20,000 range show more variation: Indonesia and Egypt cluster around 70-80%, while Cape Verde, Jordan, Palestine, and Armenia fall between 40% and 60%. Further right, in the $20,000 to $50,000 band, informal employment shares drop substantially: Mexico, Argentina, Brazil, Costa Rica, Turkey, and South Korea all register between 20% and 40%. At the far right, countries with GDP per capita above $50,000 — Spain, Germany, Russia, Serbia, North Macedonia, and Tuvalu — show informal employment shares below 20%, with several near or below 5%. The pattern visible across the chart is a consistent inverse relationship: as GDP per capita increases, the share of workers in informal employment decreases.
